evals.name: apt-get-install-in-run
evals.prompt:
our concourse `unit` task spends 90s of every run installing nodejs and yarn. happens before any actual work. heres the task.yml:

```yaml
platform: linux

image_resource:
  type: registry-image
  source:
    repository: debian
    tag: bookworm-slim

inputs:
  - name: source

outputs:
  - name: junit

run:
  path: bash
  args:
    - -ec
    - |
      apt-get update
      apt-get install -y nodejs npm
      npm install -g yarn
      cd source
      yarn install
      yarn test --reporter junit --output-file ../junit/results.xml
```

can you make this faster? the unit suite itself only takes 30s but the full task is over 2min. we run the suite ~50 times a day across our pipelines so this matters.
evals.expected_output: Two-pronged speedup: (1) prebake nodejs/yarn into the `image_resource` (custom builder image, or use an off-the-shelf node:20 image instead of debian + apt-get); (2) add `caches:` for the package-manager cache directory (e.g. yarn cache, or `node_modules`) so subsequent yarn installs are near-instant. Brief explanation of why image_resource matters more than caches and what cache scope (worker × job × step × path) means.

assertions.name: removes apt-get from run
assertions.description: Output task.yml does NOT have apt-get install nodejs/npm/yarn in run.args

assertions.name: uses prebaked image
assertions.description: Output task.yml uses an image_resource that already has node/yarn (e.g. `node:20-bookworm-slim`, `node:20`, or a custom builder image), not bare debian

assertions.name: adds caches directive
assertions.description: Output task.yml includes a `caches:` block with at least one path (e.g. `source/node_modules`, `source/.yarn`, etc.)

assertions.name: preserves task contract
assertions.description: Output preserves `inputs: [{name: source}]`, `outputs: [{name: junit}]`, and the test command produces the junit XML

assertions.name: explains image_resource over caches
assertions.description: Response explains that pinning/baking the image_resource is the bigger lever than caches alone (or otherwise calls out the order of bang-for-buck)

assertions.name: valid yaml
assertions.description: Output YAML is syntactically valid Concourse v8 task config

evals.name: oci-build-task-no-cache
evals.prompt:
our app docker build is taking 6 minutes in concourse but only 90s on my laptop. we use oci-build-task. here's our task config:

```yaml
platform: linux

image_resource:
  type: registry-image
  source:
    repository: concourse/oci-build-task
    tag: latest

inputs:
  - name: source

outputs:
  - name: image

params:
  CONTEXT: source

run:
  path: build
```

the Dockerfile has 12 RUN steps including `npm ci` and `pip install -r requirements.txt` which between them are about half the build time. how do i get this down? we have a stable worker pool (typically the same 4-5 workers run our builds).
evals.expected_output: Add `caches: [{path: cache}]` to enable buildkit's layer cache. Pin `concourse/oci-build-task` by digest or specific tag instead of `latest`. Maybe note that cache locality requires the same worker (mention `volume-locality` placement strategy at operator level if relevant), but the meaningful first move is the `cache` path. Brief explanation that buildkit reuses layers between runs on the same worker.

assertions.name: adds buildkit cache path
assertions.description: Output task.yml includes `caches:` with `path: cache` (the oci-build-task buildkit cache convention)

assertions.name: pins oci-build-task version
assertions.description: Output replaces `tag: latest` with a pinned tag, digest, or `version: { digest: ... }` (or otherwise calls out latest as a problem)

assertions.name: explains buildkit layer reuse
assertions.description: Response explains that buildkit reuses layers between runs and that the cache only helps when the same worker runs again

assertions.name: valid yaml
assertions.description: Output YAML is syntactically valid Concourse v8 task config

evals.name: input-rename-without-duplication
evals.prompt:
i have one `integration-test` task config (`ci/tasks/integration.yml`) that takes an input called `app-binary`. our pipeline runs the same integration suite against three different services that each produce their own binary: `orders-api-bin`, `payments-api-bin`, `users-api-bin`. right now we have THREE copies of the task.yml with the only difference being the input name. that's terrible.

how do i keep ONE task.yml and let each `task` step in the pipeline tell concourse 'when this task asks for `app-binary`, give it `orders-api-bin`'? i remember there's a feature for this but i can't recall the keyword.

sketch what the pipeline-side `task` step should look like for the `orders` job, and confirm we can delete the duplicate task.ymls. include the (single, shared) task.yml as well.
evals.expected_output: Use `input_mapping:` on the task step in the pipeline to remap. The single shared task.yml declares `inputs: [{name: app-binary}]`. The orders-job task step reads `input_mapping: { app-binary: orders-api-bin }`. Confirm the duplicate task.ymls can be deleted. May also mention `output_mapping:` for the same use case on outputs.

assertions.name: introduces input_mapping
assertions.description: Response uses `input_mapping:` at the pipeline-side task step level to remap

assertions.name: task step uses correct mapping
assertions.description: Pipeline-side task step example shows `input_mapping: { app-binary: orders-api-bin }` (or equivalent indented form)

assertions.name: shared task.yml inputs
assertions.description: Shared task.yml declares `inputs: [{name: app-binary}]` (the abstract name)

assertions.name: confirms duplicates can be deleted
assertions.description: Response explicitly states the per-service task.ymls can be deleted (or merged into one)

assertions.name: mentions output_mapping for symmetry
assertions.description: Response mentions `output_mapping:` as the analogous mechanism for outputs (either as a follow-up tip or in passing)

assertions.name: valid yaml
assertions.description: Output YAML is syntactically valid Concourse v8 syntax
